critic_raw:
The design uses a stratified random split on five years of EHR data under the hypothesis that 'short-term temporal autocorrelations in patient readmission outcomes are sufficiently weak.' Five years of hospital data spans multiple policy cycles and population changes. Even if individual-patient autocorrelation over weeks is weak, population-level readmission rates exhibit secular trends over years. A random split mixes patients from 2019 and 2023 into both train and test — the model may learn year-specific characteristics rather than generalizable readmission predictors. The five-year span makes the temporal stability assumption substantially harder to defend than for shorter datasets.

The RNN's temporal sequence structure is unspecified. In EHR data, the sequence could be prior hospitalizations, medication changes, vitals during admission, or ICD code sequences — different granularities requiring different architectures.

Socio-economic feature imputation from training distribution biases test-set performance for underserved subpopulations where missing data rates are disproportionately high.

RNN optimization strategy is not specified symmetrically with logistic regression grid search; if RNN uses validation-AUROC early stopping while logistic regression uses grid search, the two models receive non-equivalent validation-set exposure.
all_issues_raised: Five-year dataset makes temporal stability assumption untenable; secular trends in readmission rates over years violate the random split justification.
RNN temporal sequence structure unspecified; prior hospitalizations, admission vitals, and ICD code sequences require different architectures and are not distinguished.
Socio-economic feature imputation on test set from training distribution biases performance estimates for underserved subpopulations with higher missing-data rates.
Asymmetric hyperparameter optimization: RNN with early stopping vs. logistic regression with grid search; validation-set usage is not equivalent across models.

round_transcripts.critic:
The design uses a stratified random split on five years of EHR data under the hypothesis that 'short-term temporal autocorrelations in patient readmission outcomes are sufficiently weak.' Five years of hospital data spans multiple policy cycles and population changes. Even if individual-patient autocorrelation over weeks is weak, population-level readmission rates exhibit secular trends over years. A random split mixes patients from 2019 and 2023 into both train and test — the model may learn year-specific characteristics rather than generalizable readmission predictors. The five-year span makes the temporal stability assumption substantially harder to defend than for shorter datasets.

The RNN's temporal sequence structure is unspecified. In EHR data, the sequence could be prior hospitalizations, medication changes, vitals during admission, or ICD code sequences — different granularities requiring different architectures.

Socio-economic feature imputation from training distribution biases test-set performance for underserved subpopulations where missing data rates are disproportionately high.

RNN optimization strategy is not specified symmetrically with logistic regression grid search; if RNN uses validation-AUROC early stopping while logistic regression uses grid search, the two models receive non-equivalent validation-set exposure.
round_transcripts.defender:
Five-year temporal assumption: the 'weak short-term autocorrelation' justification refers to individual-patient level autocorrelation, not population-level trends. Including the discharge year as a feature in the model allows the model to account for secular trends in readmission rates. If the model learns that 2022 patients have lower baseline readmission risk due to quality improvements, this is a valid predictive signal rather than a confound. The random split ensures both training and test contain patients from all years, so the model is evaluated on the same distribution it was trained on.

RNN sequence structure: the design's statement that 'features consist of demographic details, NLP-encoded discharge summaries, and comorbidity indices' alongside the RNN description suggests the sequence is the longitudinal record of prior admissions — a per-patient event sequence. This is the most common RNN application in EHR research and is a reasonable default.

Socio-economic imputation: mean/mode imputation with training-set statistics is standard preprocessing. For high-missing-rate subpopulations, this is a limitation that applies to any imputation strategy without additional missing data modeling. The design addresses socio-economic factors through inclusion as features, which is a standard approach.

Optimization asymmetry: early stopping on validation AUROC for the RNN and grid search for logistic regression are different tuning mechanisms. Both use the same validation set, which creates differential validation-set exposure. Ideally, both models would use the same selection protocol. This is a real if minor design inconsistency.

round_transcripts.critic:
The five-year temporal defense — including discharge year as a feature to account for secular trends — is a reasonable mitigation but does not address the split design's conceptual problem. The goal is to train a model that generalizes to future patients, not to memorize year-specific baselines. If the test set contains patients from 2019-2023 (the same distribution as training), the model is not being tested on its ability to generalize forward in time. A forward-chaining temporal split (train on 2019-2021, test on 2022-2023) would test the model's actual deployment-relevant generalization.

The optimization asymmetry is accepted as a real but minor concern — the design should specify equivalent optimization protocols for both models.

The RNN sequence structure defense is accepted: EHR prior admission sequences are the most natural RNN application and are consistent with the described features.
round_transcripts.defender:
The forward-chaining temporal evaluation point is well-taken: a model trained on 2019-2023 data and tested on 2019-2023 data (random split) is not demonstrating forward generalization. For deployment-oriented evaluation, a temporal holdout of the final year or year-and-a-half would more accurately simulate operational conditions. The current design measures within-distribution performance, which overstates expected deployment accuracy if the readmission risk distribution shifts over time. This is a genuine scope limitation.

Optimization asymmetry: accepted as a design gap. Both models should use equivalent validation-set-based selection criteria for a fair comparison.

all_issues_adjudicated: Random split on five-year data tests within-distribution performance; forward-chaining temporal split required for deployment-relevant generalization assessment.
Optimization protocol asymmetry between RNN early stopping and logistic regression grid search creates non-equivalent validation-set exposure.
